tasks.instruction: Przeczytaj ponizszy tekst. Zdecyduj, ktore zdania (1.1.–1.6.) sa zgodne z trescia tekstu (T – True), ktore sa niezgodne (F – False), a ktore zawieraja informacje niepodane w tekscie (NI – No Information). Nastepnie znajdz w tekscie wyrazy lub wyrazenia, ktore odpowiadaja definicjom podanym w punktach 1.7.–1.11. Wymagana jest calkowita poprawnosc ortograficzna odpowiedzi.
tasks.text:
HOW I BECAME A COMMUNITY INTERPRETER

Growing up in a Polish family in Manchester, I learned early on what it means not to be understood. My grandmother would visit us for months at a time, and I would sit beside her at parents' evenings, doctor's appointments, and council meetings, turning the official words into the language she could hold onto. I was ten years old the first time I did this, and although nobody called it interpreting, that is exactly what it was.

My parents assumed I would study languages formally, but when I arrived at university I found myself drawn instead to linguistics — the science of how language works. I was fascinated not just by what words mean, but by how meaning slips and slides between cultures. A direct translation is rarely a true one. When my grandmother asked if she was "doing well" after an operation, she was asking whether she had behaved courageously; the English-speaking nurse thought she was asking about her recovery. These tiny gaps between worlds became my obsession.

After graduating, I worked for several years as a freelance translator for a legal firm in London. The pay was decent and the work was intellectually demanding, but it felt sterile — too much distance between me and the people the words were meant to serve. I missed the immediacy of real conversation and the messy, urgent reality of people trying to understand each other.

The turning point came when I volunteered to help at a refugee reception centre on the weekends. For the first time in years, my work felt as though it mattered in a direct and immediate way. I was helping people navigate a bureaucratic system in a language they barely knew, at one of the most vulnerable and frightening moments of their lives. It was exhausting and emotionally draining, but I had never felt more useful.

Within a year, I had left the law firm and retrained as a community interpreter. The money is modest compared to what I earned before, but the sense of purpose more than compensates. I now work across healthcare, education, and social services, and I train new interpreters in my spare time. If I could give one piece of advice to someone starting out, it would be this: never underestimate the weight of a word in someone else's crisis.

items.statement: The narrator first acted as an interpreter for a family member when still a child.
items.explanation: TRUE — 'I was ten years old the first time I did this', interpreting for the narrator's grandmother at various official meetings.

items.statement: The narrator studied foreign languages at university.
items.explanation: FALSE — 'My parents assumed I would study languages formally, but when I arrived at university I found myself drawn instead to linguistics', which is the science of how language works, not language study.

items.statement: The narrator found the work at the legal firm emotionally fulfilling.
items.explanation: FALSE — The narrator says it 'felt sterile — too much distance between me and the people the words were meant to serve'.

items.statement: The refugee reception centre where the narrator volunteered was run by a government organisation.
items.explanation: NO INFORMATION — The text mentions the narrator volunteered at 'a refugee reception centre' but says nothing about who ran or funded it.

items.statement: The narrator earns less money as a community interpreter than in the previous job.
items.explanation: TRUE — 'The money is modest compared to what I earned before'.

items.statement: The narrator currently works in healthcare, education, and social services.
items.explanation: TRUE — 'I now work across healthcare, education, and social services'.

vocabItems.definition: working independently, not employed by a single company
the quality of being direct and instant, without any delay or distance
related to a system of complicated official rules and procedures
tiring and using up all of one's emotional energy
to make up for something negative with something positive
